title: UI Surface Architecture
use_for: designing Unity mod UI as observer and command surface over domain/runtime state
separating UI rendering, UI state, domain state, and host UI integration
structuring windows, context panels, debug views, menu entries, and overlays
preventing UI code from becoming domain or infrastructure owner
rules: UI should read published state or view models, not transient domain internals
UI commands should call explicit application services or command boundaries
host UI insertion should be isolated from window/controller logic
debug surfaces should expose diagnostics without becoming required for correctness
UI state and domain state must remain separate
procedure: List UI surfaces: main windows, context panels, overlays, menu entries, debug views, and settings.
Define each surface's data source: published domain state, runtime diagnostics, configuration, or host context.
Define commands emitted by each surface and the boundary that handles them.
Separate host UI integration from rendering and controller logic.
Define refresh, invalidation, visibility, and input-routing rules.
Verify that UI cannot directly mutate domain internals or raw host state.
output: view state and domain state separation

primary_when: the task is primarily about UI architecture as part of a mod runtime or domain system
must_not_own_when: the task is only about styling or manual layout
the task is only about domain pipeline design

solves: how to keep UI from owning domain logic
how to make runtime and domain state visible without exposing internals
how to isolate host UI integration from feature UI

core_principles: UI is a surface over state and commands, not the source of domain truth.
Debug UI should make runtime state inspectable without changing runtime ownership.
Host UI integration is infrastructure and should be isolated from feature presentation.
View models protect UI from transient domain internals.
constraints: Do not let UI controls mutate raw host objects directly.
Do not store domain truth only in UI state.
Do not make diagnostics depend on debug windows being open.
Do not couple reusable UI architecture to a named game or project.
decision_rules: If UI displays domain results, provide published state or view models.
If UI changes behavior, route the action through a command or application service.
If UI is inserted into host menus, isolate that insertion behind a surface adapter.
If UI shows diagnostics, read from diagnostic snapshots rather than module internals.
If a window grows too large, split by surface responsibility before splitting by visual rows.

preferred_patterns: window controllers separate from render elements
avoid_patterns: host UI injection mixed with business logic
render methods calling raw game APIs
debug windows mutating internals without command boundaries
monolithic window controllers with unrelated feature ownership

recipes.name: Design a UI surface over a domain pipeline
recipes.when: Use when a UI must display runtime domain state and trigger domain commands.
steps: Define the published state the UI may read.
Create a view model or DTO that matches presentation needs.
Route UI actions through explicit commands or services.
Keep host menu or window insertion behind an adapter.
Add diagnostics views from snapshots, not internal mutable state.

pitfalls: Letting a large window controller become the application architecture.
Using UI DTOs as domain models.
Mixing host UI injection with feature decisions.
Making debug-only state part of runtime correctness.
review_checklist: Does UI read only published state or view models?
Are UI state and domain state separate?
Can diagnostics render without owning module internals?

references.title: IMGUI State Patterns
references.interpretation: Use for separating UI state from domain and runtime state.
references.why: UI surfaces should observe state without becoming domain owners.

references.title: IMGUI Composition
references.interpretation: Use for composing windows, panels, and debug surfaces.
references.why: Surface architecture needs predictable composition and ownership.
